concepts.title: Version Control with Git
concepts.body_text: Git records snapshots of your code over time so many people can change it safely and you can undo mistakes.
key_points: Commits are saved snapshots
Branches isolate work in progress
Merging combines branches

concepts.title: Code Review & Pull Requests
concepts.body_text: A pull request proposes changes so teammates can read, comment on and approve them before they join the main code.
key_points: Catches bugs before release
Spreads knowledge across the team
Keeps a discussion trail

concepts.title: Automated Testing
concepts.body_text: Tests are extra code that checks your real code still behaves correctly, run automatically on every change.
key_points: Unit tests check small pieces
Tests catch regressions early
Green tests build confidence to change code

concepts.title: Clean Code & Refactoring
concepts.body_text: Refactoring improves the shape of code (names, structure) without changing what it does, keeping it easy to read and extend.
key_points: Clear names beat clever tricks
Small functions are easier to test
Refactor under green tests

concepts.title: Arrays & Hash Maps
concepts.body_text: An array stores items in a numbered row; a hash map stores items by a key so you can fetch them almost instantly.
key_points: Arrays: fast by position
Hash maps: fast by key
Choice depends on how you look data up

concepts.title: Big-O / Time Complexity
concepts.body_text: Big-O describes how the work a piece of code does grows as the input gets bigger, ignoring constant details.
key_points: O(1) constant, O(n) linear, O(n^2) quadratic
Predicts behaviour at scale
Guides which approach to pick

concepts.title: Tables, Keys & SQL Basics
concepts.body_text: Relational databases store data in tables of rows and columns; keys link tables and SQL is the language used to ask questions of the data.
key_points: Primary key uniquely identifies a row
Foreign key references another table
SELECT/INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE are core SQL

concepts.title: Indexing
concepts.body_text: An index is a sorted lookup structure that lets the database find rows without scanning the whole table.
key_points: Speeds up reads, slows down writes
Works like a book's index
Pick columns you filter/join on

concepts.title: Transactions & ACID
concepts.body_text: A transaction groups several changes so they all succeed or all fail together; ACID names the guarantees that keep data correct.
key_points: Atomic: all-or-nothing
Consistent, Isolated, Durable
Prevents half-finished updates

concepts.title: Star Schema
concepts.body_text: A star schema models data as a central fact table of measurements surrounded by dimension tables that describe them, optimised for analytics.
key_points: Fact table holds measures and foreign keys
Dimensions hold descriptive attributes
Denormalised for fast reads

concepts.title: Slowly Changing Dimensions
concepts.body_text: SCD techniques decide how to handle a dimension attribute that changes over time (overwrite, keep history, or store the previous value).
key_points: Type 1 overwrites, keeps no history
Type 2 adds a new row per change
Type 3 keeps a previous-value column

concepts.title: Normalization vs Denormalization
concepts.body_text: Normalization splits data to avoid duplication; denormalization deliberately duplicates it to make reads faster.
key_points: Normalize for clean writes
Denormalize for fast reads
Warehouses often denormalize

concepts.title: Batch vs Streaming
concepts.body_text: Batch processing handles a bounded chunk of data on a schedule; streaming processes never-ending data continuously with low latency.
key_points: Batch favours throughput
Streaming favours latency
Many systems use both (lambda/kappa)

concepts.title: Idempotency
concepts.body_text: An idempotent step produces the same result no matter how many times it runs, so retries and backfills are safe.
key_points: Use deterministic keys and upserts
Critical for exactly-once semantics
Makes failures recoverable

concepts.title: DAGs & Schedulers
concepts.body_text: A DAG (directed acyclic graph) describes tasks and their order; a scheduler like Airflow runs them on time and in the right sequence.
key_points: Nodes are tasks, edges are dependencies
Acyclic = no loops
Schedulers handle retries and timing

concepts.title: Data Quality & Validation
concepts.body_text: Data quality checks verify incoming data is complete, valid and reasonable before it is trusted downstream.
key_points: Check nulls, ranges, uniqueness
Fail fast on bad data
Freshness matters as much as correctness

concepts.title: What dbt Is & the T in ELT
concepts.body_text: dbt is a transformation tool that lets you build data models as SQL SELECT statements; it owns the 'T' in ELT, running inside your warehouse after raw data has been loaded.
key_points: Transforms data already in the warehouse (ELT, not ETL)
Models are just SELECT statements dbt turns into tables and views
Brings software-engineering practices to analytics SQL

concepts.title: dbt Projects, Profiles & Targets
concepts.body_text: A dbt project is a folder of models and configuration (dbt_project.yml); profiles.yml stores the warehouse connection, and a target chooses which environment (dev or prod) a run uses.
key_points: dbt_project.yml configures the project; profiles.yml holds credentials
Targets switch between dev and prod connections
Keep secrets out of the project with environment variables

concepts.title: Models & the ref() Function
concepts.body_text: A model is a .sql file containing one SELECT statement; the ref() function references another model so dbt can build them in the right order and draw the lineage graph.
key_points: One model equals one SELECT equals one table or view
ref() creates dependencies and the build order
Never hard-code table names; always ref() other models

concepts.title: Sources & the source() Function
concepts.body_text: Sources declare the raw tables loaded into your warehouse so models can reference them with source(); they also enable freshness checks and document where data first enters dbt.
key_points: Declare raw tables once in a sources YAML file
source() references raw data the way ref() references models
Source freshness checks catch stale upstream loads

concepts.title: Materializations
concepts.body_text: A materialization decides how dbt persists a model's result: a view (saved query), a table (full rebuild), ephemeral (inlined CTE), or incremental (append new rows).
key_points: view is cheap storage, recomputed on read
table gives fast reads but is rebuilt each run
Set materialized in config or dbt_project.yml

concepts.title: Incremental Models
concepts.body_text: Incremental models build a table once, then on later runs insert or update only new rows, using is_incremental() and a unique_key to avoid reprocessing the whole dataset.
key_points: Process only new or changed rows after the first run
is_incremental() guards the filter logic
unique_key drives merge and upsert behaviour

concepts.title: Project Structure: Staging, Intermediate & Marts
concepts.body_text: A common dbt layout layers models as staging (clean raw sources one-to-one), intermediate (reusable business logic), and marts (final analytics tables), keeping large projects readable.
key_points: Staging cleans and renames raw columns
Intermediate holds shared transformation steps
Marts are the final star-schema tables users query

concepts.title: Jinja Templating in dbt
concepts.body_text: dbt compiles SQL through Jinja, a templating language that adds variables, loops, and if-statements so you can generate repetitive SQL instead of copying it by hand.
key_points: Double braces print values; brace-percent runs logic
Loops generate repetitive SQL automatically
dbt compiles Jinja plus SQL into plain SQL before running

concepts.title: Macros & Packages
concepts.body_text: A macro is a reusable Jinja function that returns SQL; packages bundle macros and models from others (installed with dbt deps) so you can share and reuse logic across projects.
key_points: Macros are like functions for SQL, keeping it DRY
Install community packages with dbt deps
dbt_utils is the most widely used package

concepts.title: Seeds
concepts.body_text: Seeds are small CSV files in your project that dbt loads into the warehouse as tables with dbt seed, ideal for static lookup data like country codes or category mappings.
key_points: Version-controlled CSVs become warehouse tables
Best for small, rarely-changing reference data
Not for large or frequently-updated data

concepts.title: Snapshots & SCD Type 2
concepts.body_text: Snapshots record how a row in a source table changes over time, implementing Slowly Changing Dimension Type 2 by stamping validity columns each time tracked values change.
key_points: Capture the history of mutable source rows
Implements SCD Type 2 with valid_from and valid_to
timestamp and check strategies detect changes

concepts.title: Tests
concepts.body_text: dbt tests are assertions about your data: generic tests (unique, not_null, accepted_values, relationships) declared in YAML, and singular tests written as SQL that should return zero rows.
key_points: Generic tests are reusable one-line YAML checks
Singular tests are SQL queries that must return no rows
A failing test stops the pipeline before bad data ships

concepts.title: Documentation & Lineage
concepts.body_text: dbt generates a documentation site from your YAML descriptions and model code, including an interactive lineage graph that shows how every table depends on the ones before it.
key_points: Describe models and columns in YAML
dbt docs generate builds a browsable site
The lineage DAG visualises end-to-end dependencies

concepts.title: The dbt build Workflow
concepts.body_text: dbt build runs models, tests, snapshots, and seeds together in dependency order, stopping a path when a test fails; selection syntax lets you run just the part of the DAG you changed.
key_points: build equals run plus test plus snapshot plus seed in DAG order
Selectors run only the models you changed
Failed tests block their downstream models

concepts.title: Deployment, Environments & CI
concepts.body_text: In production dbt runs on a schedule against a prod target, often in dbt Cloud or an orchestrator; CI runs your models and tests on every pull request to catch breakage before merge.
key_points: Separate dev and prod targets and datasets
Schedule production runs via dbt Cloud or Airflow
CI builds and tests changed models on each pull request
